titleStmt.title: Polymer Network Diffusion in Charged Gels

funder.orgName: Japan Society for the Promotion of Science

analytic.title: Polymer Network Diffusion in Charged Gels

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: The swelling kinetics of charged polymer gels reflect the complex competition among elastic, mixing, and ionic contributions. Here, we used dynamic light scattering to investigate the collective diffusion coefficient of model gels, whose polymer network structure was controlled so that the three contributions were comparable. We demonstrate that the collective diffusion coefficient stems from the sum of elastic, mixing, and ionic contributions, without evident cross-correlations. The significant ionic contribution conforms to the Donnan equilibrium, which explains equilibrium electrical potential gradients in biological systems.

Charged gels, characterized by ionic species immobilized in the polymer network, exhibit enhanced swelling compared to electrically neutral gels because of the excess osmotic pressure caused by the Donnan effect [1,2]. Their remarkable ability to swell up to 1000 times their dry weight has facilitated their use as superabsorbent polymers for diapers, horticultural water retention agents, and self-healing concrete. This has led to extensive investigation of their swelling behaviors [3][4][5][6][7].

Swelling kinetics are crucial for practical applications of these gels. Tanaka and co-workers [8,9] modeled the swelling kinetics of electrically neutral polymer gels, introducing the collective diffusion coefficient D of a polymer network as

where K is the osmotic bulk modulus, G is the shear modulus, and f is the friction coefficient (per unit volume) between polymer networks and solvents. However, quantifying D based on Eq. (1) remains challenging [10] due to difficulties in estimating K and f . Moreover, G ≪ K is often assumed, leading to D = K/f [9], even though this is an oversimplification. Equation (1) extends to charged gels by including ionic contribution to K from Donnan equilibrium [1][2][3]. However, validation of this approach is complicated due to the complex competition among polymer-solvent mixing, elastic, and ionic contributions. To date, only a few scaling laws between D and the polymer volume fraction ϕ have been experimentally examined, including D ∼ ϕ 1/2 at low salt concentrations [11][12][13] and D ∼ ϕ 2/3 at high salt concentrations [14]. The main obstacle in experimental validation arises from the difficulty in controlling polymer networks. Consequently, studies often use natural polymers like xanthan, chitosan, and hyaluronic acid, which lack precise control over network structure, have been typically used, limiting accurate gel dynamics research.

Recently, we have developed a strategy to synthesize tunable model polymer networks [15] and identified the equations governing gel elasticity, including negative energy elasticity [16,17], as well as the equation of state of osmotic pressure [18,19]. We have also quantitatively validated Eq. ( 1) for neutral gels [20][21][22][23] This Letter extends the relationship D = D mix + D el to charged gels, introducing an ionic contribution D ion :
div.formula: D = D mix + D el + D ion .(2)
div.p: To validate Eq. ( 2), we experimentally investigated D in charged gels with comparable D mix , D el , and D ion via dynamic light scattering (DLS). We controlled the molar concentrations of immobilized charge in the gel at the as-prepared state c 0 im and equilibrated the gels in various outer salt solutions with diverse molar concentrations of mobile ions c out [Fig. 1(a)]. Our results indicate that the significant contribution of D ion to D is explained by the excess osmotic pressure from the Donnan equilibrium [1,2]. These findings demonstrate the successful prediction of dynamic property from the static properties of charged gels, suggesting broader applicability of this relationship to charged gels in good solvents.

Theoretical derivation of collective diffusion coefficient in charged gels.-We derive the contribution of D ion to D, using a similar procedure as that for D mix and D el in neutral gels [20][21][22][23]. The osmotic bulk modulus K ≡ c(∂Π/∂c) in Eq. ( 1) is quantified by total osmotic pressure Π, where c is the polymer mass concentrations. For charged gels, Π is considered as the sum of polymer-solvent mixing Π mix , elastic Π el , and ionic Π ion contributions [24][25][26][27][28][29][30] as Π = Π mix + Π el + Π ion , where Π mix ∼ c 3ν/(3ν-1) [18,19] and Π el = -G 0 (c/c 0 ) 1/3 [31,32]. Here, ν ≈ 0.588 is the excluded volume parameter [24], and G 0 and c 0 is the shear modulus and polymer mass concentration, respectively, at the as-prepared state. Further, the friction coefficient per unit volume f in Eq. (1) follows f ∼ c 3/2 [21,22,33]. Notably, Π mix and f depend only on c at a constant T within the same polymer-solvent system, regardless of the polymer network structures [18,21,22,33]. Assuming same dependences of Π mix , Π el , and f in charged gels, we can express each contribution to D using Eq. (1) as
div.formula: D mix = 3ν 3ν -1 Π mix f , D el = G f ,(3)
div.p: and

The D ion originates from Π ion produced by immobilized charge in polymer networks. Notably, Eqs. (3) have been validated for neutral gels at as-prepared states [20,21,23] and swollen states [22]. We quantified Π ion using the Donnan equilibrium [1][2][3]. For a polymer network with immobilized charge molar concentration c im in a solution containing monovalent ions, the molar concentrations of mobile cations and anions inside the gel (c + and c -) and in the outer salt solution (c + out and c - out ) satisfy the following electric neutrality conditions: (i) c im + c + = c -for the inner gel, (ii) c out ≡ c + out = c - out for the outer salt solution, and (iii) c + c -= (c out ) 2 between inner gel and outer salt solution. Combining the conditions (i) and (iii) yields c ± = 1 2 c im √ 1 + 4α 2 ∓ 1 , where α ≡ c out /c im (≥ 0) is the molar concentration ratio between the mobile ion in the outer salt solution and the immobilized charge in the polymer network. Hence, the osmotic pressure due to the immobilized charge is
div.formula: Π ion = RT (c + + c --2c out ) = c im RT 1 + 4α 2 -2α .
div.p: (5) Substituting Eq. ( 5) into Eq. ( 4) gives
div.formula: D ion = c im RT f √ 1 + 4α 2 . (6
div.p: According to Eq. ( 6), α regulates the efficiency of D ion , with D ion = c im RT /f in the charged gel limit (α ≪ 1) and D ion = 0 in the neutral gel limit (α ≫ 1). Materials and methods.-As a model system to investigate charged gels, we used a tetra-arm poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) hydrogel, synthesized via the AB -type crossend coupling of two prepolymers (tetra-arm PEG) of equal size. Each end of the tetra-arm PEG was modified with a mutually reactive amine (tetra-PEG-NH 2 ) and succinimidyl ester (tetra-PEG-OSu) (NOF Co., Tokyo, Japan). All other reagents were purchased from WAKO Pure Chemicals (Osaka, Japan). All materials were used without further purification. We dissolved tetra-PEG-NH 2 and tetra-PEG-OSu (molar mass 11 kg/mol) in phosphate buffer (pH = 7.0, 60 mM) to achieve a concentration of c = 60 g/L.

To fabricate charged gels, we mixed two solutions at stoichiometrically imbalanced ratios of s = 0.325, 0.350, 0.375, and 0.400 (Table I), where s is the molar fraction of minor tetra-PEG polymers ([tetra-PEG-OSu]) to total tetra-PEG polymers ([tetra-PEG-NH 2 ] + [tetra-PEG-OSu]). An excess tetra-PEG-NH 2 (s > 0.5) allowed some amine groups to remain partly unreacted [34], serving as the immobilized cations in the polymer networks [see Fig. 1(b)]. We kept each sample in an enclosed space to maintain humid conditions at room temperature (T ≈ 298K) for reaction completion.

We immersed each charged gel into the outer salt (HCl + NaCl) solutions of pH = 4.0 and c out = 0.1-30 mM (Table I) to completely protonate immobilized cations and to tune the parameter α ≡ c out /c im in Eq. ( 6). We replaced the outer salt solution twice every 20 hours to achieve equilibrium swollen state. The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation [35,36] determined the dissociation constant of immobilized residues as K a ≈ 1.0 × 10 -10 M (see Supplemental Material, Sec. S1 [37]), confirming complete protonation of amines in all experimental conditions because K a was smaller than [H + ] = 10 -4 M.

We measured the diameter d of each gel at equilibrium in the outer salt solutions, using an optical microscope (M165 C; Leica, Wetzlar, Germany) to calculate the volume swelling ratio Q = (d/d 0 ) 3 (initial diameter d 0 = 1.04 mm), determining c = c 0 /Q and c im = c 0 im (c/c 0 ). To ensure accurate c 0 and c 0 im values at each s, we assessed the sol fraction eluted out from the polymer network during the swelling process using the Bethe approximation [37-39] (Further details are described in Supplemental Material, Sec. S2 [37]).

We measured D of charged gels via DLS (ALV/CGS-3 compact goniometer, Langen, Germany) in the same way as Refs. [20][21][22][23]. Each gel sample was fabricated in a scattering glass tube (disposable culture tube 9830-1007 with an inner diameter of 8.4 mm; IWAKI, Japan) and was swollen in the outer salt solution. We carefully selected d 0 of each gel to prevent contact between a swollen gel and an inner wall of the glass tube. Once equilibrium was reached, we measured the scattering light intensity I(t) at time t over 600 s at a scattering wavelength λ = 632.8 nm and a scattering angle θ = π/2, to evaluate the autocorrelation function [40,41] g (2) (τ ) ≡ ⟨I(0)I(τ )⟩/⟨I(0)⟩ 2 for delay time τ ≈ 0.01-0.1 ms, corresponding to the thermal fluctuation of a polymer network [20-23, 42, 43] (results are provided in Supplemental Material, Sec. S3 [37]). Here, ⟨⟩ denotes the time-average. We fitted the autocorrelation functions using a stretched exponential function g 2 (τ ) -1 = A exp[-2(τ /τ g ) B ] + ϵ, where A is the initial amplitude, B is the stretched exponent, and ϵ is the timeindependent background. Using the partial heterodyne model [12,44], we evaluate D = (1 + √ 1 + A)/(q 2 τ g ), where q = (4πn/λ) sin(θ/2) ≈ 0.0187 nm -1 is the scattering vector with the refractive index n ≈ 1.333 of the aqueous solvents.

We measured the (equilibrium) shear modulus of gels at the equilibrium swollen state G for each c out using a rheometer (MCR302; Anton Paar, Graz, Austria) with a 25-mm parallel plate geometry. The gels, shaped into discs with a height 1 mm and a diameter 35 mm, were equilibrated in outer salt solutions with varying c out (Table I). We measured the storage modulus G ′ and loss modulus G ′′ at T = 298 K with angular frequency ω = 0.63-63 rad/s and shear strain γ = 1.0%, as well as ω = 6.3 rad/s and γ = 0.1% -10%. For all samples, G ′ was independent of γ and ω, and was much larger than G ′′ (Supplemental Material, Sec. S4 [37]). Hence, we selected G ′ measured at ω = 6.3 rad/s and γ = 1.0% as the (equilibrium) shear modulus G. Using G = G 0 (c/c 0 ) 1/3 for swollen gels [31,32] (see Supplemental Material, Sec. S5 [37]), we evaluated G 0 at the as-prepared state as G 0 = G(c 0 /c) 1/3 for each s (Table I).

Estimating D mix and D el in neutral gels.-Before considering charged gels, we briefly revisit the decomposition of mixing (D mix ) and elastic (D el ) contributions to D in a model neutral gels, based on our previous studies [20][21][22][23]. Figure 2(a) shows the collective diffusion coefficient measured via DLS D DLS in neutral gels at the as-prepared state, plotted against G 0 for various s. The observed linear relationship between D DLS and G 0 is consistent with Eqs. (3). Notably, D mix is independent of s, because Π mix and f is independent of s [18,21,22,33]. Thus, we can decompose D DLS into the mixing contribution D mix = lim G→0 D DLS and the elastic contribution D el = D DLS -D mix [see Fig. 2(a)].

Through the decomposition of D mix and D el , we obtain the empirical formulas D mix = 2.2 × 10 -12 c 0.8 and f = 8.0×10 11 c 1.5 for neutral gels (the detailed results are provided in Supplemental Material, Sec. S7), corroborating the scaling predictions f ∼ c 1.5 [45] and Π mix ∼ c 2.3 [46,47] (for gels in a good solvent). These formulas are applicable to neutral gels at the as-prepared and swollen states, as confirmed in our previous study [22]. Using values of c and G, we can accurately calculate D for a neutral gel. Figure 2(b) demonstrates that the collective diffusion coefficient of neutral gels measured via DLS (D DLS ) shows excellent agreement with Eqs. (3), using empirical formulas of D mix and f .

Estimating D ion in charged gels.-We expand our analysis to electrically charged gels and examine Eq. ( 2), by hypothesizing that the formulas of D mix and D el , as established for neutral gels, remain applicable. Figure 3 shows D DLS for swollen neutral and charged gels (equilibrated in outer salt solutions with varying c out ), as measured via DLS. For varying s samples, a decrease in s corresponded with an increase in c 0 im and a decrease in G 0 . This resulted in an increase in Q to be a decrease in c (= c 0 /Q) in outer salt solutions (Table I). The decrease in G 0 is attributed to a decrease in elastically effective subchains in polymer networks [15]. Moreover, for a fixed s, a decrease in c out in outer salt solutions led to a lower α (≡ c out /c im ), resulting in increased Q to be decreased c (= c 0 /Q) (Table I).

For neutral gels [Fig. 3(a)], D DLS agrees with D mix + D el , which is independent of c out (see Supplemental Material, Sec. S8 [37] for further details). For charged gels with each s [Fig. 3(b) and(c)], we calculate D mix (gray region) and D el (blue region), using the same empirical formulas for neutral gels with all measurable G 0 and Q. Here, we experimentally confirmed that G = G 0 (c/c 0 ) 1/3 holds for charged gels (see Supplemental Material, Sec. S5 [37]), indicating that the immobilized charge does not significantly affect the elastic modulus. At high c out (α ≫ 1), D DLS approaches the neutral gel limit (D mix + D el ), because D ion → 0 in Eq. ( 6). In contrast, at low c out (α ≪ 1), D DLS deviates from the neutral gel limit (D = D mix + D el ), indicating a significant D ion to D (indicated by red arrows), which contributed up to approximately half of D at its maximum. We show that D of charged gels can be accurately described by simply adding D ion in Eq. ( 6). Figure 4 demonstrates that the collective diffusion coefficient of charged gels measured via DLS (D DLS ) shows excellent agreement with the predictions of Eqs. ( 3) and ( 4), using the same empirical formulas of D mix and f as in neutral, and applying Eq. ( 6) with each c im and α. These findings suggest that Eq. ( 1) can be extended to include charged gels by simply adding D ion without evident crosscorrelations. Notably, Eq. ( 6) is applicable in weakly charged gels and is less effective in polyelectrolyte gels with high c im , due to counterion condensation [7,48].

Concluding remarks.-We developed model charged gels [Fig. 1 andTable I] with comparable D mix , D el , and D ion and measured their collective diffusion coefficient D via DLS. Based on the same decomposition of D mix and D el to D in neutral gels [Figs. 2 and3], our results indicate that D in the charged gel is governed by Eq. ( 1), where D ion emerges as an additive component [Fig. 4]. The significant ionic contribution D ion to D is quantitatively described through the ionic osmotic pressure Π ion [Eqs. ( 5) and ( 6)], which originates from the Donnan equilibrium.

Our findings demonstrate the successful prediction of the dynamic behaviors from the static properties of charged gels equilibrated in a good solvent. This aspect is particularly relevant considering the dynamics in gel-like biological systems such as extracellular matrices, which are predominantly negatively charged [49] and influenced by the Donnan effect. Therefore, an in-depth understanding of the dynamics in charged gels will enhance our comprehension of the behaviors of biological systems. )], where ρ water ≈ 997 kg/m 3 is the density of water. Using W polymer and W I. The measured c 0,exp is in good agreement with c 0 , supporting the validity of Bethe approximation.

div.head: III. AUTOCORRELATION FUNCTION MEASURED VIA DYNAMIC LIGHT SCATTERING
div.p: Figure S2 shows typical results of the autocorrelation functions for charged gels in outer salt solutions measured via dynamic light scattering. We fit each autocorrelation function using the stretched exponential function g 2 (τ )-1 = A exp[-2(τ /τ g ) B ] + ϵ, with the fitting parameters listed in Table II. We evaluate D = (1 + √ 1 + A)/(q 2 τ g ), using partial heterodyne model [S5, S6].

div.head: IV. DYNACMIC VISCOELATICITY MEASUREMENTS OF CHARGED GEL IMMERSED IN OUTER SALT SOLUTIONS
div.p: Figure S3 shows typical results of strain (γ) and frequency (ω) dependencies of storage (G ′ ) and loss (G ′′ ) moduli in charged gels in outer salt solutions measured via rheometry. The G ′ (ω) is within the linear response region and is independent of the frequency (ω) below 100 rad/s. We regard G ′ (ω) 1 rad/s as the shear modulus G, because the equilibrium shear modulus is given by G = lim ω→0 G ′ (ω).

div.head: V. DECREASE OF SHEAR MODULUS G DURING SWELLING IN CHARGED GELS
div.p: We demonstrate the decrease of shear modulus G = G 0 (c/c 0 ) 1/3 during the swelling process for charged gels. This nonlinear relationship is consistent with models proposed by Flory [S7] and Panyukov [S8], which consider the competition of dilution and pre-extension of the polymer network due to swelling. Figure S4 shows the normalized elastic modulus G/G 0 as a function of c 0 /c. All results are consistent with G/G 0 = (c/c 0 ) 1/3 , indicating that the immobilized charge does not significantly influence the elastic modulus in charged gels.

div.head: VIII. EFFECT OF SALT CONCENTRATION ON COLLECTIVE DIFFUSION COEFFICIENT IN NEUTRAL GELS
div.p: We investigate the effect of c out on D in a neutral gel. Figure S7 demonstrates that D DLS (= D mix + D el ), as measured via dynamic light scattering, remains unaffected by c out within the experimental range of this study (c out = 0.1-30 mM). The data for D DLS at s = 0.500 presented in Fig. 3(a) in the main text is derived from the average of D DLS shown in Fig. S7. [S1] L. J. Henderson, Concerning the relationship between the strength of acids and their capacity to preserve neutrality, Am. J. Physiol. Content 21, 173 (1908).

figure.head: FIG. 1 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic illustration of the swelling of a charged gel. The charged gel with immobilized charge cim is equilibrated in the outer salt solution, whose mobile ion cout is equivalent to that of cations and anions: cout = c + out = c - out . The mobile cations and anions inside the gels (c + and c -) follow the Donnan equilibrium [1-3]: c + c -= (cout) 2 . (b) Schematic illustration of charged gels with precisely controlled polymer networks. The unreacted end functional groups yield the immobilized charge in the gel at the as-prepared state c 0 im with the decrease of the shear modulus G0, which is controlled by nonstoichiometrically mixing two types of precursors in an AB -type polymerization system.

figure.head: DFIG. 2 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2.Collective diffusion coefficient for neutral gels. (a) Typical result of shear modulus (G0) dependence of the collective diffusion coefficient and its components (Dmix and D el ) for neutral gels at the as-prepared state. We tune s at the constant c so that D el can be controlled independently. (b) Comparison between the collective diffusion coefficients measured via DLS (DDLS) and calculated by Eqs. (3) (Dmix + D el ) for neutral gels, using Dmix = 2.2 × 10 -12 c 0.8 and D el = G0/(8.0 × 10 11 c 1.5 ). The solid line represents DDLS = Dmix + D el . In (a) and (b), the data of neutral gels for c = 30, 60, 90, and 120 g/L with the molar mass 20 kg/mol at s = 0.35, 0.4, 0.45, and 0.5 are taken from Ref.[23].

figure.head: FIG. 3 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3. Polymer mass concentration (c) dependence of the collective diffusion coefficient and its components (Dmix and D el ) for neutral gels at (a) s = 0.500 and charged gels for varying cout at (b) s = 0.400 and (c) s = 0.325. The c 0 im increases as s decreases (black dotted arrow). For charged gels, Dmix = 2.2×10 -12 c 0.8 and D el = G0(c/c0) 1/3 /(8.0×10 11 c 1.5 ) were assumed to be equal to neutral gels. The results at s = 0.375 and s = 0.350 are shown in Supplemental Material, Sec. S6 [37].

figure.head: DFIG. 4 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 4. Comparison between the collective diffusion coefficients measured via DLS (DDLS) and calculated by Eqs. (3) and (4) (Dmix + D el + Dion) for charged gels. Here, Dmix and D el were assumed to be equal in neutral gels: Dmix = 2.2 × 10 -12 c 0.8 and D el = G0(c/c0) 1/3 /(8.0 × 10 11 c 1.5 ), and Dion was calculated by Eq. (6) with each cim and α. The solid line represents DDLS = Dmix + D el + Dion.

figure.head: 1 τ
figure.figDesc: FIG. S2. Autocorrelation functions [g(2) (τ ) -1] of charged gel s = 0.375 in outer salt solutions with varying cout = 0.10-30 mM measured via dynamic light scattering at T = 298 K. Each solid curve is a least-squares fit of the stretched exponential function.

figure.head: c
figure.figDesc: FIG. S3. Storage modulus G ′ loss modulus ′′ of charged gels at s = 0.375 in outer salt solutions with varying cout = 0.10-30 mM measured via rheometry at 298 K. (a) γ-dependence at ω = 6.3 rad/s and (b) ω-dependence at γ = 1.0%.

figure.head: FIG. S4 .
figure.label: S4
figure.figDesc: FIG. S4. Dependence of G/G0 on c/c0 in charged gels with varying s. The error bars represent the experimental errors. The solid line represents G/G0 = (c/c0) 1/3 .

figure.head: 14 Ns/m 4 )
figure.figDesc: FIG. S5. Polymer mass concentration (c) dependences of Dmix and f evaluated through the decomposition of D in neutral gels [Fig. 2(a) in the main text]. The error bars represent the standard error. Each solid line represents the empirical formulas Dmix = 2.2 × 10 -12 c 0.8 and f = 8.0 × 10 11 c 1.5 .

FIG. S6. Polymer mass concentration dependences of collective diffusion coefficient and its components (Dmix and D el ) in charged gels at (a) s = 0.400, (b) s = 0.375, (c) s = 0.350, and (d) s = 0.325 for varying cout.

figure.head: FIG. S7 .
figure.label: S7
figure.figDesc: FIG. S7. Dependence of DDLS measured via dynamic light scattering on cout in a neutral gel at s = 0.500. The dotted line represents the average of DDLS.

[S2] K. A. Hasselbalch, The calculation of the hydrogen content in blood from free and combined carbonic acid, and the oxygen compound of the blood as the function of the hydrogen content, Biochem. Z. 78, 112 (1917). [S3] C. W. Macosko and D. R. Miller, A new derivation of average molecular weights of nonlinear polymers, Macromolecules 9, 199 (1976).

figure.head: TABLE I .
figure.label: I
figure.figDesc: Synthetic conditions of charged gels (s, c0, and c 0 im ) and experimental conditions for immersing charged gels in outer salt solutions (cout). We evaluated α ≡ cout/cim and measured G0 and Q for each gel sample.

row.cell: c0 (g/L) c 0 im (mM) cout (mM) cout/cim G0 (kPa) Q
. Least-square fitting parameters of stretched ex-
ponential function applied to autocorrelation functions in

div.p: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS This work was supported by the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) through the Grantsin-Aid for JSPS Research Fellows Grant No. 19J22561 to T.F. and No. 202214177 to T.Y., Scientific Research (B) Grant No. 22H01187 to N.S., Scientific Research (A) Grant No. 21H04688 to T.S., Transformative Research Area Grant No. 20H05733 to T.S., and MEXT Program Grant No. JPMXP1122714694 to T.S. This work was also supported by JST through CREST Grant No. JP-MJCR1992 and Moon-shot R&D Grant No. 1125941 to T.S.

div.p: Supplemental Material for: "Polymer Network Diffusion in Charged Gels"

Shoei Sano, 1 Takashi Yasuda, 1 Takeshi Fujiyabu, 1 Naoyuki Sakumichi, 2, * and Takamasa Sakai We determine the dissociation constant (K a ) of the amine group on the ends of the prepolymer (tetra-PEG-NH 2 ) using an automatic titration apparatus (COM-1700A; HIRANUMA, Ibaraki, Japan). We added HCl solutions of 10 mM to tetra-PEG-NH 2 solution for c = 10 g/L at T = 298 K to neutralize the amine groups (NH + 3 to NH 2 ). Figure S1 shows the decrease in pH with increasing titer (V ), indicating d(pH)/dV shows a sharp peak at V ≈ 2.85 mL. Using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation [S1, S2] for the point where
Accordingly, we estimated pK a ≈ 10.0, yielding K a ≈ 1.0 × 10 -10 M.

div.head: II. EVALUATION OF SOL FRACTION THROUGH BETHE APPROXIMATION
div.p: To accurately determine c 0 and c 0 im of polymer gels immersed in outer solutions, we evaluate the sol fraction * Correspondence should be addressed to N. Sakumichi or T. Sakai: sakumichi@gel.t.u-tokyo.ac.jp † sakai@gel.t.u-tokyo.ac.jp eluting from the polymer network during the swelling process using the Bethe approximation [S3, S4]. Considering the AB -type end coupling between tetra-functional polymers (A4 and B4), the Bethe approximation gives the probability that one arm of each polymer does not connect to the infinite network [P (F out A ) and P (F out B )] as
(pre) 0
is the polymer mass concentration at preimmersion, and
) where M is the molar mass of prepolymers.

To validate Eqs. (S3) and (S4), we experimentally measured the polymer mass concentration immersed in outer solutions c 0,exp at each s and compared with c 0 evaluated through the Bethe approximation [Eq. ( S3)]. We measured the weight of a gel at pre-immersion W (pre) 0 and immersed a gel in pure water for 48 hours. This process was repeated twice to completely remove the sol fraction. Subsequently, we dried the gel at T = 298 K for 48 hours and measured the weight W polymer . Here, the weight of polymers in the gel at pre-immersion is W (pre) polymer =
